system_prompt:
You are distilling one chapter of a guitar method book into a short summary.

The user will give you a chapter file with topic-organized verbatim quotes
plus the human-rationale notes for each. Write a one-paragraph summary (4-7
sentences) that captures:

  - what this chapter teaches (the techniques, concepts, rules, examples)
  - the master's characteristic moves visible in this chapter (if any)
  - the load-bearing terminology this chapter introduces

Reference the topic labels from the chapter file when useful. Do not invent
information not present in the chapter. If the chapter is biographical or
introductory rather than instructional, say so explicitly.

Output just the paragraph — no preamble, no JSON, no markdown formatting.
user_prompt:
Chapter 8: Quartals, Inner-Voice Motion, Scales and Comping Texture (Lessons 43-50)

<<<CHAPTER FILE
# Chapter 8 — Quartals, Inner-Voice Motion, Scales and Comping Texture (Lessons 43-50)

_Pages 89-104._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Perfect 4th on guitar strings

> most neighboring pairs of strings are tuned a perfect 4th apart (all except strings 3 and 2, the G and B strings, which are a
> major 3rd apart). That is, if you play a note at the same fret of two different strings, next to each other, you've got a perfect 4th,
> unless you're on the second and third strings together.

**Significance (p. 89).** Physical tuning logic for quartal playing.

## Quartal fingering strategy

> Multiple notes at the same fret will likely require some rolling of the fingers (looks like barring, but we try to get
> only one tone at once), and position shifts are needed along the way as well.

**Significance (p. 89).** Finger-rolling technique for quartal lines.

## Quartal outside playing pattern

> The pattern, starting at the beginning of that measure, could be described as going down two perfect 4ths in a row from our
> starting point and repeating that whole three-note unit a minor 3rd further down each time (from A now instead of from C,
> and so on). Notice that right as we repeat the unit, moving from D up to A to do so, we get a perfect 5th interval, which is the
> inversion of a perfect 4th (D down to A would've been a 4th). So in a way, this plan simply leads to a continual stream of the
> same interval.

**Significance (p. 89).** Generative mechanic for outside quartal pattern.

## Inner-voice motion: rootless voicings

> Throughout, we're using mainly voicings that seem like familiar chord shapes with the usual root on the bottom
> omitted (from the A or low E strings), which can be helpful for mobility. A couple of them actually have the root
> included on top. In a band setting, the bass generally covers these chordal roots anyway, and leaving them off is often
> a good way to vary the texture, even when we're not trying to move around so much.

**Significance (p. 90).** Rootless voicings: mobility + ensemble role.

## Sus4 inner-voice device

> In measures 2 and 3, 7 and B+7 are both given a brief sus4 treatment, letting the chordal 3rd move up a half step to
> the 4th and back, for a momentary 7sus4 sound.

**Significance (p. 90).** Half-step 3rd-to-4th suspension as inner-voice device.

## #9 / b9 interchangeability

> 749 and 729 chords are often interchangeable, and late in measure 4 we take advantage of that principle, using first a
> B:7#925 voicing and right after it a B-7b9#5. This creates some melodic motion from the 29th tone to the +9th on the high
> E string.

**Significance (p. 90).** #9/b9 interchange used for inner-voice melodic motion.

## Scales as starting point, not ceiling

> The trouble is, scales do not tell the whole story when it comes to improvising—not even about note choice. They can initially
> free us up by giving us a place to start that at least doesn’t sound bad, but then trap us into a box of limitations if we consider
> any one scale to be the total “how-to” of playing on a certain chord or progression.

**Significance (p. 92).** Central warning: scales are a starting tool, not a method.

## Lydian dominant vs. Mixolydian

> An alternative to the Mixolydian scale, for fitting a natural dominant chord, could be the Lydian dominant scale, or the
> Mixolydian with a raised 4th degree, which avoids the potential clash of the natural 4th sustained against the chord (and also
> accommodates a #11th, the lone alteration that'll fit in with this type of dominant seven).

**Significance (p. 93).** When to prefer Lydian dominant over Mixolydian.

## Comping density as dynamic tool

> In this lesson, we will focus mainly on how'we can vary the density, and with it the intensity, of our comping to fit different
> musical situations or to be better equipped to build and come down in dynamics through the course of a tune.

**Significance (p. 94).** Textural density as primary dynamic lever.

## Sparse comping: 3rds and 7ths only

> These tones are all 3rds or 7ths of the chords (except for the E in measure 1, which is the 6th of G major). We shouldn't forget
> that if we want to allow the texture to be on the thin side, we could go as far as to lay out entirely while a soloist holds forth,
> at least for a while!

**Significance (p. 94).** Guide-tone-only sparse comping + laying-out extension.

## Simple ideas over complex changes

> These short phrases are made up almost entirely of B» major scale tones (which are, in fact, all within B+ major pentatonic),
> aside from the blues-flavored C#/D>. We could also analyze these tones vis-a-vis the chords and say that the D towards the end
> of measure 2 implies an F13 and that the D> in measure 4 makes it an F7>13 (or F745). However, both by its sound and by how
> we'd find it on the fretboard, it makes even more sense to just call it a groovy little mostly-B}-major idea that works!

**Significance (p. 96).** Pentatonic key-of-tune approach trumps chord-by-chord analysis.

## Transcription: transposing ii-V-I lines

> The whole idea could be transposed to different keys for use in all kinds of songs. If we wanted to use it for a ii-V-I in Bb major,
> for example, we could simply move the whole thing down seven frets on the guitar—or learn to play it on a different string
> set.

**Significance (p. 99).** Fret-shift transposition turns transcription into vocabulary.

## Swing feel: three practical tips

> } Play behind the beat: By this we mean to lay back on it on purpose, in a hip, often relaxed kind of way, which is
> different than dragging or falling behind.
>
> ) Play 8th notes straight to make them swing: This is especially good advice if someone is over-exaggerating the
> swung timing, but probably not too literally true, else we could never meaningfully talk of swung 8ths! It’s a useful
> thought to play around with in any case, perhaps related to the next idea...
>
> » Accent the upbeats: A general assumption in music might be to put more emphasis on the notes that fall on
> downbeats, right with the pulse of the tune, but a jazzer might at least even this out a bit with some accent on the
> “ands” in an 8th-note flow. While involving tone and dynamics, this has an impact on the rhythmic feel.

**Significance (p. 100).** Three named swing-feel techniques with distinctions.

## Feel vs. notated rhythm

> Please be aware that this is not the same thing as creating variations of the essential rhythmic figure (anticipating notes,
> switching 8ths and quarters, adding triplets or rests, etc-—that is a valuable, but different, exercise). We're aiming here for
> variations that would, if written again on paper, look the same as what we had in the first place. These are explorations of the
> feel with which one basic idea can be played.

**Significance (p. 101).** Distinguishes rhythmic variation from feel variation.

## Ballad rhythmic subdivision

> the ballad feel often entails
> a particular mix of even 8ths and triplets for the underlying division, which can be heard in all the instruments of the
> band.

**Significance (p. 102).** Defining duple+triple subdivision of ballad feel.

## Ballad comping: arpeggiated touch

> We may allow ourselves a more arpeggiated touch on a few chords in this situation, more so than we usually would at a
> swinging tempo. That is, they may be sounded with a looser sweep across the strings, as at the end of measure 2 and in
> measure 5 (think “brrring” instead of “chung"), or played with their notes stacking up in a distinct rhythm as in the beginnings
> of measures 2 and 3.

**Significance (p. 102).** Arpeggiated chord attack for ballads, brrring/chung target.

CHAPTER FILE>>>
